metadata.title: Arranging the fruit basket: A computational approach towards a better understanding of adolescents' diet-related social media communications

abstract: This study investigated the contents and linguistic styles that adolescents use in their dietary communications on social media. The main aim of the study was to describe adolescents' social media communication practices with regards to diet, in order to better understand dietary social influences and how to effectively intervene in them. The current study made use of an online communication dataset (N = 72,384 messages) of adolescents in primary and secondary school (N = 1038, 9-16 y/o). We analyzed the message content using tailor-made diet-and health-related dictionaries and LDA topic modeling. The linguistic style of the messages was investigated by assessing the sentiment and underlying psychological dimensions. Further, we used multivariate linear regressions to test how each message dimension was related to message liking, which was considered as an indicator of a message's influence potential regarding peers' dietary behaviors. The results confirmed that adolescents show a preference to discuss neutral-to-unhealthy dietary items most often. No association emerged between message content (i.e., message healthiness and topics) and the number of likes that a message received, while messages with more positive sentiment and higher subjectivity received slightly more likes. Also, our findings confirm that visual dietary content is more often liked than textual descriptions alone. The findings indicate that the content and the healthiness of a message does not explain its popularity among adolescents. Rather, the way how diet-related content is formulated (i.e., in a positive and subjective tone) emerged as a more important predictor for message liking.

paragraphs: Obesity is one of the biggest threats to global public health in the 21st century. Global obesity rates have risen linearly over the past decades and there is no end in sight (Lee, 2019;OECD, 2022). Particularly worrisome is the sharp increase in childhood obesity, setting youth on a path to the early development of type-2 diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular issues (CBS, 2019;Caprio et al., 2020). This development is not only detrimental to the individuals themselves; it also places a serious burden on national health care systems and economies (Schofield et al., 2017;Wolf & Colditz, 2012). There is overwhelming evidence that poor dietary behaviors (i.e., overconsumption of low-nutrient high-calorie foods and drinks and a corresponding lack of nutrient-dense low-calorie foods and drinks) are major drivers of this development (see, e.g., Greger, 2020;Koyuncuoglu Güngör, 2014;Roberts & Barnard, 2005). Dietary behaviors tend to be formed early in life and are hard to change later on (Bandura, 2004). To reduce the spread of the global obesity pandemic, a better understanding is needed of what causes these poor dietary behaviors in adolescents (i.e., 8-18-year-olds).

Adolescents' social environment is one of the most important determinants of their dietary behaviors, which has typically been explained by mechanisms of social influence (Bandura, 2008;Cruwys et al., 2015;Salvy et al., 2012). Social influence is defined as behavioral or attitudinal change due to the demands of the social environment (Cialdini, 2008). Besides parents as a critical source of dietary social influence (Feunekes et al., 1998;Lau et al., 1990), the peer group assumes importance in adolescence and increasingly becomes a reference point for social norms (Beazidou & Botsoglou, 2016;Birch, 1980). In a process called peer socialization, adolescents tend to adjust their behaviors to the present norms in the peer group that they want to belong to (Albert et al., 2013;Andrews et al., 2020;Henneberger et al., 2021). Possible reasons for this might be adolescents sensitivity, both to the possibility of social reward (Brechwald & Prinstein, 2011), as well as the risk of social exclusion (Blakemore, 2018).

Several theories and models have been proposed that explain peer influences on dietary behavior. One way that adolescents learn dietary behaviors from peers is through observation, which is often referred to as behavior modeling (Bandura, 2008). It is well established that adolescents tend to emulate the dietary practices they observe around them (Bandura, 2008;Birch, 1980;Cruwys et al., 2015;Vartanian et al., 2015). Social influence is also manifested in social norms, with peer groups acting as a normative reference point, determining which dietary behavior is considered normal and which behaviors are expected of the individual (Bevelander et al., 2012;Higgs, 2015;Salvy et al., 2012). Social network studies have laid out how these social influences might drive the spread of obesity through social ties (Christakis & Fowler, 2007;de la Haye et al., 2013). Christakis and Fowler's (2007) large-scale study found that individuals' risk of becoming obese increased substantially as the rate of obesity increased in their close social environment (i.e., among their friends, relatives, or spouses). Indeed, it has been demonstrated that over time, adolescents and their peers converge in terms of their dietary behavior (de la Haye et al., 2013) and their body mass index (BMI; Powell et al., 2015).

paragraphs: Today's adolescents are almost ubiquitously connected through their social media networks, where they are exposed to a vast amount of dietary influences from friends, influencers, and industry (Chung et al., 2021;Folkvord et al., 2021;Guadagno et al., 2013;Vaterlaus et al., 2015). For example, Buzzfeed's Facebook page Tasty (~ 100 million followers) and the Instagram hashtag #food (> 200 million posts) are two of the most popular pages/hashtags on their respective networks. Moreover, adolescents are exposed to numerous attempts of food marketing from mostly unhealthy food brands (Folkvord et al., 2016(Folkvord et al., , 2021;;Qutteina et al., 2019). But also, adolescents themselves engage in dietary content creation. Content analyses have found that 85 % of adolescents share posts, depicting some kind of dietary items on social media (Holmberg et al., 2016). The ubiquity of dietary content online is notable, given that the consumption of diet-related content online has been found to shape short-term (Hawkins et al., 2021;Qutteina et al., 2021), and long-term (Lizie, 2012;Signorielli & Staples, 1997) dietary attitudes and behaviors. Behavioral effects are likely explained by the beforementioned social influence pathways such as behavior modeling and social norms (Centola, 2013;Gruzd & Wellman, 2014).

Despite the importance of dietary social media communication as a potential source of influence, the literature on adolescents' dietary social media communication has remained scarce. The lack of knowledge about what and how adolescents preferably communicate about diet, hinders our understanding of dietary social influence online, as well as our ability to effectively intervene in those structures. For example, online social network interventions, despite being extremely promising to address a youth population, have struggled to generate long-term engagement in their audiences (Klassen et al., 2018b;Maher et al., 2014;Yang, 2017). The authors highlight the importance of understanding audiences' communication preferences around health, in order to engage them in online interventions. The present study aimed to address this gap by describing adolescents' dietary social media communications along two dimensions: their content and linguistic style. Moreover, the study aims to describe which message contents and linguistic styles are most appreciated (i.e., liked) by adolescents. Although it is beyond the scope of this paper to measure actual dietary behavior as the outcome variable, message liking will give an indication of a message's likelihood to be influential. This likelihood for social influence will be referred to as a message's influence potential from here on.

Anlyses of adolescents dietary social media content have shown that adolescents more often communicate about unhealthy, non-core foods (Barre et al., 2016;Holmberg et al., 2016). Adolescents' dietary communication behavior hereby resembles the food-related posts that they themselves are exposed to. Adolescents' online environments have been found to promote unhealthy over more healthy dietary norms, for example through an abundance of food marketing or social media influencers (Folkvord et al., 2021). Over 70 % of adolescents have been found to engage with branded food content on social media, for example through the creation of user-generated content (Fleming-Milici & Harris, 2020). Given the quantity of dietary influences, it is not surprising that dietary social media posts created by adolescents have been found to resemble food advertisements, portraying unhealthy and branded foods in a fun and attractive manner (Holmberg et al., 2016). This is problematic, given that these posts establish an unhealthy dietary norm within adolescents' social media networks. Moreover, the healthiness of online dietary communication has been shown to be predictive for obesity risk in the user population (Abbar et al., 2015;Blackburn et al., 2018;Fried et al., 2014). Both referenced foods within a message, and on a more abstract level the broader topic of dietary messages (e.g., the standard American diet, Mexican food, or vegetarian), have been found to be strongly associated with rates of diabetes and obesity rates (Fried et al., 2014).

The linguistics style of a message (i.e., how the content is communicated) has been shown to be indispensable for understanding message context and possible message effects (Abbar et al., 2015;Huang et al., 2018;Pennebaker et al., 2003). Two of the most studied dimensions of linguistic style are message sentiment and a message's latent psychological dimensions. In the field of dietary communication, message sentiment has been used as a reliable measure to assess the valence of attitudes that users held towards specific types of food or dietary categories (e.g., Ahmed et al., 2021;Mostafa, 2019;Zhou & Zhang, 2017). Holmberg et al. (2016) found that adolescents predominantly communicate about unhealthy, branded foods in a positive tone. Although to a lesser degree, the study also found a positive framing for healthy foods, highlighting the palatability and homemade character of these meals. Message sentiment can therefore be a relevant marker for food enjoyment (de Ridder & Gillebaart, 2022), giving important insights into the context in which those foods are discussed.

The psychological dimensions of a message are often measured using the Linguistic Inquiry Word Count (LIWC) tool (Pennebaker et al., 2015). LIWC has been used in hundreds of studies to analyze which psychological processes underly communication data (including affection, socialness, and cognitive, perceptual, or biological focus). On the one hand, psychological measures have been used to gain relevant insights about the message authors. For example, LIWC has been used to compare adolescents' social media communication styles with those of adults, finding that adolescents communicate from a more personal subjective (vs. factual) perspective (Siriaraya et al., 2011), while communicating their emotions more often (Jang et al., 2016). Also, psychological dimensions have been studied in the context of social media engagement (Veltri & Atanasova, 2015). For example, psychological dimensions (as measured by LIWC) showed promising results in elucidating the role of different emotions in retweeting behavior (e.g., Kim & Yoo, 2012;Mahmud et al., 2014;Stieglitz & Dang-Xuan, 2012). Hwong et al. (2017) used LWIC's psychological dimensions to predict the rate of engagement (sharing, comments, likes) with Facebook and Twitter posts with up to 90 % accuracy. In sum, assessing the linguistic style of a message (i.e., message sentiment and LIWC's psychological dimensions) not only can enrich the understanding of message context but also the understanding of how and why people engage with dietary messages.

In addition, we introduce the concept of influence potential, which, in the present context, refers to the likelihood that a message might influence users' dietary attitudes or behaviors. On social media, influence potential is strongly linked to the concept of social validation, for example, users' engagement with the content (Guadagno et al., 2013). Message engagement (i.e., message liking and sharing) signals a positive attitude towards the content, and in a broader sense, establishes visible social norms regarding the liked (or not liked) content. Twitter messages with more social validation (i.e., message likes), have been rated as more credible and more persuasive when they had already been validated by peers (Hamshaw et al., 2018;Lutfeali et al., 2020). Hawkins et al. (2021) demonstrated that the viewing of socially endorsed (i.e., highly liked) diet-related content caused the presented food to be consumed more thereafter. These results coincide with results from brain imaging studies, showing that posts with more likes, were associated with stronger activation of neural reward, social cognitions, imitation, and attention pathways (Sherman et al., 2016). Based on these findings, the present study used the number of likes that a message received as a measure of its influence potential. We infer that the higher the number of likes that a message received, the more likely it was that the message was influential in impacting dietary attitudes or behaviors.

For example, psychological dimensions (as measured by LIWC) showed promising results in elucidating the role of different emotions in retweeting behavior (e.g., Kim & Yoo, 2012;Mahmud et al., 2014;Stieglitz & Dang-Xuan, 2012). Hwong et al. (2017) used LWIC's psychological dimensions to predict the rate of engagement (sharing, comments, likes) with Facebook and Twitter posts with up to 90 % accuracy. In sum, assessing the linguistic style of a message (i.e., message sentiment and LIWC's psychological dimensions) not only can enrich the understanding of message context but also the understanding of how and why people engage with dietary messages.

In addition, we introduce the concept of influence potential, which, in the present context, refers to the likelihood that a message might influence users' dietary attitudes or behaviors. On social media, influence potential is strongly linked to the concept of social validation, for example, users' engagement with the content (Guadagno et al., 2013). Message engagement (i.e., message liking and sharing) signals a positive attitude towards the content, and in a broader sense, establishes visible social norms regarding the liked (or not liked) content. Twitter messages with more social validation (i.e., message likes), have been rated as more credible and more persuasive when they had already been validated by peers (Hamshaw et al., 2018;Lutfeali et al., 2020). Hawkins et al. (2021) demonstrated that the viewing of socially endorsed (i.e., highly liked) diet-related content caused the presented food to be consumed more thereafter. These results coincide with results from brain imaging studies, showing that posts with more likes, were associated with stronger activation of neural reward, social cognitions, imitation, and attention pathways (Sherman et al., 2016). Based on these findings, the present study used the number of likes that a message received as a measure of its influence potential. We infer that the higher the number of likes that a message received, the more likely it was that the message was influential in impacting dietary attitudes or behaviors.
sections.preview_head: Today's adolescents are almost ubiquitously connected through their social media networks, where they are exposed to a vast amount of dietary influences from friends, influencers, and industry (Chung et al., 2021;Folkvord et al., 2021;Guadagno et al., 2013;Vaterlaus et al., 2015). For example, Buzzfeed's Facebook page Tasty (~ 100 million followers) and the Instagram hashtag #food (> 200 million posts) are two of the most popular pages/hashtags on their respective networks. Moreover, adolescents are exposed to numerous attempts of food marketing from mostly unhealthy food brands (Folkvord et al., 2016(Folkvord et al., , 2021;;Qutteina et al., 2019).

paragraphs: Currently little is known about the precise message characteristics of adolescents' diet-related social media communications. The present study aimed to address this gap by using a previously collected dataset to investigate the content (i.e., message healthiness and dietary topics) and the linguistic styles (sentiment and psychological dimensions) adolescents used in their diet-related social media communications. Further, the study investigated the relationship between content, linguistic style, and influence potential of diet-related messages. Describing how message characteristics might be related to a higher influence potential is the first step to understanding (unhealthy) dietary influence dynamics in adolescents' online networks. This information is critical for engaging adolescents more effectively, i.e. in social media health interventions (Klassen et al., 2018b;Maher et al., 2014;Yang, 2017).

Previous studies have found that a large majority of adolescents posts about dietary topics on their social media feeds (Holmberg et al., 2016;Qutteina et al., 2019) without specifying the concrete share of dietary, compared to non-dietary postings on the social media platform. This makes it hard to gauge the intensity of dietary influence that adolescents are exposed to. For this reason, the study first aimed to quantify the amount of dietary content within the dataset:
RQ1a. What was the proportion of dietary messages relative to the total quantity of communication on adolescents' social media?

Second, the study aimed to reveal the content of adolescents' dietary communications by assessing the healthiness of foods and drinks referenced within them. Previous studies have assessed the healthiness of social media messages in rather broad terms, either coded by food group, core foods (vs. non-core food), or through calory count of referenced foods (Fried et al., 2014;Holmberg et al., 2016;Qutteina et al., 2019). Each of these strategies has advantages and drawbacks. On the one hand, the classification via core/non-core foods might lable "cheese" as a core food, without capturing a need for moderation. On the other hand, classification via caloric count might label "nuts" as unhealthy food on par with high-caloric processed food. To more accurately describe the healthiness of referenced foods, the study used the nutriscore labeling system, which has proven to be a reliable measure of nutritional quality (Dréano-Trécant et al., 2020;Finkelstein et al., 2019;Ter Borg et al., 2021):

RQ1b. What was the healthiness level of the adolescents' diet-related communications, based on the nutri-scores of referenced dietary items? Third, to describe message content on a more abstract level, the study aimed to investigate broader dietary topics in adolescents' dietary communications. A similar attempt to categorize dietary content on social media has been carried out for visual dietary content (Holmberg et al., 2016;Qutteina et al., 2019). The current study aims to advance these findings, by categorizing dietary messages based on their textual contents.:

RQ1c. What were the broader latent topics in the youths' diet-related social media messages? Message sentiment and latent psychological dimensions were assessed as two dimensions of linguistic style. Although some research has discussed the type of dietary content that adolescents share on social media, how this content is communicated has been typically neglected. This is particularly problematic with regards to computational text analyses, as it can struggle to identify context in textual data. Assessing message sentiment was critical to elucidate the context in which diet is discussed (e.g., differentiating between fast food being discussed with a positive vs. a negative sentiment). Assessing common psychological dimensions (e.g., if diet was mentioned in an affectionate vs. nonaffectionate or social vs. non-social context) we aimed to get even more detailed insights into message context and adolescents' communication preferences regarding diet.
RQ2a. What was the linguistic style (i.e., message sentiment, subjectivity, affection, socialness, cognitive, perceptual, or biological focus, and drive) of the adolescents' diet-related messages?

RQ2b. How did the linguistic style of a message differ for different levels of message healthiness? Finally, to investigate the relations between different message characteristics and the influence potential of a message, message likes were used as an indicator of the message's influence potential. We aimed to contribute to the literature by identifying linguistic dimensions that might be associated with engagement and potentially influence outcomes. In the light of previous research, two assumptions were made: First, it has been shown that social media messages that are emotionally charged generally perform better in terms of message engagement (Stieglitz & Dang-Xuan, 2012). More precisely, recent studies suggest that particularly content that creates positive emotion in the viewer is more strongly associated with messages liking (Klassen et al., 2018a;de Oliveira Santini et al., 2020). Multiple studies have reported that adolescents generally favor unhealthier food and beverages over more healthy dietary items (Barre et al., 2016;Holmberg et al., 2016). Moreover, it has been found that adolescents are particularly inclined to engage with unhealthy (branded) food content on social media (Fleming-Milici & Harris, 2020;Folkvord et al., 2021). Given this research, we assume that adolescents' preference for unhealthy dietary items will be reflected in their message liking. Given the outlined considerations, the following hypotheses were proposed: H1. There is a negative association between the healthiness of dietrelated messages and the number of likes messages receive.

Schlicht et al. - 2022 - Arranging the fruit basket A computational approach towards a better understanding of adolescents'
paragraphs: The current study made use of a social media communication dataset, stemming from the MyMovez project (Bevelander et al., 2018). The MyMovez project received ethical approval of the European Research Council's Ethical review board (project nr. ERC-COG-617253) and the host institution's Ethics Committee of Social Sciences (ECSW2014100614 -222). In a longitudinal study design, the project examined health behavior of Dutch adolescents. In the course of 3 years, 17 primary school and 11 secondary school classes participated in one or more of the seven data collection waves. Children were recruited through their schools regarding a program for a healthy lifestyle. School principals provided permission to conduct the research in the respective school classes. Upon providing information about the content of te program, children's caretakers and children provided active consent for participation in the study. Children without active consent were not allowed to participate in the study (see Bevelander et al. (2018) for a more detailed description).

The dataset encompasses 72,384 unique social media messages, exchanged between February 2017 and July 2018 by Dutch adolescents in primary and secondary school (N = 1038, 9-16 y/o, 53 % female). Upon data collection all message had been anonymized, therefore leaving no traces of person-identifiable data. The mean age of message authors was 11.23 years (SD = 1.41). Messages were exchanged on a tailor-made smartphone application, via a social platform called the Social Buzz. Social Buzz was pre-installed on smartphones that were handed out to all children as part of a longitudinal research project on adolescents' health behaviors. The social media platform was introduced to the kids as a feature that they could make use of to communicate with their friends, which was however not mandatory. Also, during the instruction participants were informed that the messages were moderated by the project team and messages were not private. In case of inproper use (e.g., cursing or bullying), participants could be blocked from the social platform. On the platform, the adolescents were able to send direct messages to each other or communicate via group chats with peers, similar to popular instant messaging apps like What-sApp or Facebook messenger. Also, adolescents were able to exchange images and memes from a preselected and curated image collection that covered a wide array of different topics. Moreover, adolescents were able to like messages from their peers. For primary school children that predominantly did not own a smartphone before the study, the communication over Social Buzz oftentimes represented their primary channel of social media communication with peers. Particularly these children showed high enthusiasm during the introduction of the social media platform. Secondary school children indicated that they used other social media platforms, besides the one provided by the researchers. Overall, participants sent between 1 and 221 messages per day, indicating an overall good adoption of the platform only lying slightly below the amount of teens' daily texting in real life (Lenhart et al., 2010).

paragraphs: For the present study, we decided to use a computational approach to the text analysis. On the one hand, this choice was made considering the size of the dataset, which comprised of over 400.000 messages. Computational data processing presented a time-effective way to filter and create a sample of unique, diet-related messages. On the other hand, we are aware that computational text analysis is growing, though still an emerging methodology in social sciences (Boumans & Trilling, 2016;van Atteveldt et al., 2019). By using computational text analysis, we aimed to gain empirical, but also methodological insights, that can be used in the scope of even bigger datasets. Hereby we aim to contribute to the emerging paradigm of automated, data-driven health communication, by assessing the usefulness of different linguistic data for health communication purposes.

Hereby we aim to contribute to the emerging paradigm of automated, data-driven health communication, by assessing the usefulness of different linguistic data for health communication purposes.

paragraphs: To subset messages that contained dietary words, this study made use of a custom-built diet-related word dictionary. As a starting point, diet-related words were retrieved from specific diet-related online lexica used in previous research (e.g., Sharma & De Choudhury, 2015) (EnchantedLearning, n.d.;Lingolex, n.d.). To account for regional characteristics of the sample, diet-related words (foods, brand, and meals) that are particularly popular within the Netherlands were added (e.g., Hollandforyou, n.d.;Tasteatlas, 2021). Because most of the scraped websites were in the English language, the collected words were translated into Dutch using the deepl.com online translator. The translation was proofread by a native speaker, also filtering out words that could have a double meaning that was not related to diet. Subsequently, a word embedding model (trained on ~600 million Dutch social media messages, Nieuwenhuisje, 2018) was run on each word of the dictionary. The word embedding model outputs a list of synonyms, that is typically used in a similar semantic context as the inputted word. For example, word embedding results for the word "McDonalds" indicated that it was often referred to as "Maccie" by native social media users. But also popular misspellings of words were captured by the model. Synonyms that were diet-related and not yet present within the dictionary were added to it (n = 146). Finally, for each word within the dictionary a stemmed version of this word was added using the snowball stemmer package (Snowball, n.d.). Consequently, the dictionary both picked up on the actual dietary word, and its word stem. This provided the added benefit, that we were able even pick up misspelled words, as long as their word-stem was still intact. The full dictionary (N = 1943) included the following diet-related categories: foods (e.g., "cucumber", "salami", "salmon"), meals (e.g., "pizza", "carbonara", "nasi goreng"), beverages (e.g., "water", "soda", "tea"), eating-related words (e.g., "eating", "hunger", "breakfast"), popular restaurant chains (e.g., "McDonald's", "KFC", "Starbucks"), supermarkets (e.g., "Albert Heijn", "Lidl", "ALDI"), dietary brands (e.g., "Coca Cola", "Oreo", "Maggi"), and food-related internet jargon (e.g., "mjamm", "yummie", "foodporn"). The full dictionary list can be retrieved from the projects' Github repository (MovezNetwork [GitHub], 2022).

The dietary dictionary was used to filter out messages that contained at least one diet-related word. Inspecting a random subsample of 100 messages, the obtained results were highly accurate with the large majority of messages (95 %), actually discussing diet-related content. A small subset of messages used dietary words, however in a non-dietrelated context (e.g., "jij bent een perzik " [you are a peach] or "cookie clicker", which referred to a video game). These instances were hard to remove by computational means and remained as noise within the data. Given the rareness of these instances, they were considered unlikely to bias the results in a meaningful manner.

paragraphs: To obtain the best results from computational natural language processing (NLP), it is critical to clean the communication data before analysis (Boumans & Trilling, 2016). The data-cleaning process in the current study followed five steps.

First, stop words that were not informative in terms of the dietary content of a message were removed, including personal pronouns (e.g., "I", "you") and conjunctive words (e.g., "and", "or"). For the same reason, all punctuation was removed from the texts. Second, because messages in the dataset were written by young adolescents, many messages contained misspellings and online slang, for example, due to participants stretching out the vowels of a word (e.g., "Hiiiii). Words with more than two vowels in a row are extremely rare in the Dutch language. Therefore, all occurrences of three vowels in a row were changed to two vowels instead. An exception was the vowel "i", where double occurrences were corrected to single occurrences instead. Third, to standardize the remaining text all words were lowercased and tokenized. Tokenization splits a sentence into its single word component, allowing to analyze each word of a sentence as s single entity. Fourth, single-word tokens were lemmatized using the pattern.nl lemmatizer which is specialized for the Dutch language (Smedt & Daelemans, 2012). Lemmatizing reduced each word to its word stem and gave insight into the linguistic function (e.g., noun, verb adjective, adverb) of the word within the sentence.

For topic modeling, some additional data cleaning steps were performed to optimize the interpretability of the topics. Rarely used words in the dataset (words occurring only once) were filtered out, since they indicated either a misspelling or an extremely rare word, which often adds noise to the topic models (Prabhakaran, 2018). Moreover, it is common practice to only keep the most characteristic words of a sentence for topic modeling. Therefore, only nouns, verbs, and adjectives were retained. Given that exchanged messages were oftentimes very short, this left us with an average message length of 3.03 words per message (SD = 4.58).

paragraphs: Message healthiness was measured using the nutri-score labeling system, which ranges from A (very healthy) to E (very unhealthy). The nutri-score system was transformed into a five-point scale from 1 (very healthy) to 5 (very unhealthy). In a first step, a nutri-score was calculated for every single dietary word (food, drink, or meal) mentioned in adolescents' online messages. The nutri-score was calculated using nutritional values (i.e., fat, saturated fat, carbohydrates, protein, sugar, salt) collected from the world's largest verified nutrition database (Nutritionix, n.d.). Nutritional values can differ for a single search quest, for example, the nutritional values for "pizza" differ, depending on if it is a homemade or a store-bought pizza. To address this divergence, we averaged the first five results on Nutrionix for each dietary item. This generally resulted in both homemade and store-bought scores being equally considered. On the flip side, this averaging led to a small regression to the mean effect, with extreme values becoming less likely. Subsequently, the averaged dietary values were translated into one nutri-score per food/drink item in the dictionary (Colruyt, n.d.).

In a second step, the healthiness of the entire message was calculated by averaging all nutri-scores within it (e.g., 4 and 5 -> 4.5). Again, this led to small regressions towards the mean. Further, the variable was centered and reverse coded, such that values ranged from -2 (very unhealthy) to 2 (very healthy). Messages that did not mention any dietrelated words did not have a nutri-score and were therefore coded as missing values. This resulted in a subsample of 1008 messages with a healthiness score. The final sample size was deemed sufficient for all planned parts of the data analysis (messages sentiment, LIWC, topic modeling) (RPubs, n.d.; AWS, n.d.). Therefore we proceeded with the analysis as planned.

paragraphs: To identify broader diet-related topics in adolescents' messages, we used Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA), and specifically the gensim LDA package (Gensim, 2018;Řehůřek & Sojka, 2011). LDA requires to specify the number of topics before analysis, related to the number of topic clusters that it aims to find. The identification of the ideal number of topics is often done via researchers' judgment (How coherent and interpretable is the topic to the researchers?), but can also be quantified using topic coherence analysis, which outputs one metric of topic coherence (Kapadia, 2020). Topic coherence refers to the degree that common words within a topic show semantic similarity. This measure assures, that the words identified by LDA share a semantic context, instead of being statistical artifacts. An example of words with coherent shared semantic context would be: "Game", "played", "sports", "ball". In the present study, we combined both approaches, using topic coherence as a guideline, but trusting on researcher judgment of semantic context for the final decision.

In order to assess a metric for topic coherence, a topic coherence analysis was run for varying numbers of topics (from k = 0 to k = 65). The results showed that the strength of topic coherence leveled out at k = 45, and stayed on a stable level thereafter), showing that coherence neither improved nor decreased with a higher number of topics. At face validity setting the number of topics at k = 50 resulted in the most coherent topic clusters (topic coherence = 0.69) and was therefore chosen. Alpha and eta scores were estimated using the default "auto" parameter. Since randomness is an inherent feature of LDA topic modeling, it will produce different results every time the analysis is run. To allow for reproducibility the random_state parameter was therefore fixed to an arbitrary number (random_state = 42). The LDA model was trained using a Tf-idf representation (term frequency-inverse document frequency). The Tf-idf representation provides a measure of originality, comparing the number of times a word appears in the document, with the number of documents it appears in. Using the Tf-idf distribution noticeably improved the topic interpretability, compared to a regular representation.

sections.preview_tail: The LDA model was trained using a Tf-idf representation (term frequency-inverse document frequency). The Tf-idf representation provides a measure of originality, comparing the number of times a word appears in the document, with the number of documents it appears in. Using the Tf-idf distribution noticeably improved the topic interpretability, compared to a regular representation.

paragraphs: To determine message sentiment, the study made use of the pattern. nl Python package (Smedt & Daelemans, 2012), which is specialized for the Dutch language. The tool outputs two metrics per message: The sentiment metric (-1: completely negative, 0: neutral, 1: completely positive; M = 0.25, SD = 0.47) measures the valence of the message's tone. For messages like "Lekker pizza eten" [Eating delicious pizza], the tool output a positive score of 0.60, while the sentiment score for "vies fruit hebben we" [We have gross fruit] was at a negative -0.75. The subjectivity metric (0: completely objective, 1: completely subjective; M = 0.78, SD = 0.22) indicates if the message sentiment is a subjective statement of opinion, or rather a description of factual information. While a personal statement like "Ik heb sphaghetti met boerenkool paprika kip knoflook en walnoten gegeten rare combinatie maar wel lekker." [I ate sphaghetti with kale paprika chicken garlic and walnuts strange combination but tasty] scored the highest score of subjectivity (1.00), a factual message like "Ik ben thee aan het maken" [I am making tea], scored 0. Messages that did not contain any word indicative of sentiment or subjectivity received a zero score from the pattern.nl package (71 % of the sample). This was oftentimes due to messages in the sample being very short on average, therefore not always containing sentiment-related words (M Words per Message = 8.51, SD = 13.73).

paragraphs: Latent psychological dimensions within the messages were assessed, using the 2015 version of the LIWC tool (Pennebaker et al., 2015) ranging from 0 (no congruence) to 100 (high congruence). More specifically, all six psychological summary variables that are tapped by LIWC were selected for the analysis: Affection (indicative words: e.g., "tasty", "awful"), socialness (e.g., "mate", "talk"), cognitive focus ("cause", "rather than"), perceptual focus ("look", "hear"), biological focus ("eat", "hunger") and drives ("danger", "benefit"). Affection indicates the presence of emotion, without sensitivity to valence (e. For each psychological dimension, the LIWC tool outputs one score per message, depending on the congruence between words in the message and words within the respective LIWC-sub-dictionary. A low number reflects less congruence (e.g., with affective words), while high numbers reflect a high level of congruence.

paragraphs: The study aimed to control for the most important between-subjects differences. Sex and age have been shown to covary with both dietrelated behaviors and social media engagement behaviors. For example, it has been demonstrated that girls tend to be more healthconscious than boys, which is linked to different consumption patterns of dietary information on (social) media (Fagerli & Wandel, 1999;Nelson & Fleming, 2019). Moreover, the age of children was added as a covariate, as age-related cognitive development might play a role in childrens' susceptibility to persuasion attempts, for example regarding the ability for delayed gratification (Folkvord et al., 2021;Klump et al., 2000).

paragraphs: It has been suggested that message length can be a predictor of persuasive outcomes (Chaiken, 1987). The "length implies strength"heuristic states that longer messages are generally perceived to be more convincing. However, it is unknown if this rule applies to social media conversations of adolescents. Therefore, the relation between message length (measured in words per message) and message likes was assessed. Also, it is known that images and textual information are processed via two separate cognitive pathways (Defeyter et al., 2009;Rose, 2016), for example with images being more effortlessly processed. The analysis explored the relation between messages containing an attached image (vs. no image) and message liking. Further, the role of the image's content was investigated, differentiating between images depicting dietrelated and non-diet-related content.

sections.paper_id: Schlicht et al. - 2022 - Arranging the fruit basket A computational approach towards a better understanding of adolescents'
paragraphs: Corresponding to the size of the dataset, the study took a computational approach, using natural language processing. The analysis followed three analytical steps. First, it investigated the content of dietrelated messages by (RQ1a) calculating the percentage of diet-related messages that contained at least one diet-related word, (RQ1b) calculating the average healthiness score per message based on the nutriscores of mentioned dietary items, and (RQ1c) using LDA to synthesize broader topics of youth's dietary-related messages.

Second, the linguistic style of messages was assessed by using the pattern.nl package in Python and the LIWC tool (Pennebaker et al., 2015;Smedt & Daelemans, 2012). More specifically the pattern.nl package was used to assess message sentiment and subjectivity (RQ2a). Further, the psychological dimensions of messages were assessed, by measuring six psychological summary variables from the LIWC package (RQ2b). A bivariate correlation analysis investigated whether specific linguistic styles (i.e., sentiment or psychological dimensions) were associated with different levels of message healthiness (RQ2c).

Third, we used regression analysis to investigate the relations between message content, linguistic style, and message likes (H1-2 and RQ3a-b). Due to the lack of previous research, we aimed to single out the explanatory power for each separate message dimension (message healthiness, message topics, message sentiment and subjectivity, psychological dimensions). Because the various dimensions were not mutually exclusive, we ran four separate regression analyses with message likes as the dependent variable. Finally, an exploratory regression analysis investigated the interplay between all message dimensions and message liking. This allowed for a direct comparison between all independent variables in one model. Also, we explored the role of other potential predictors of message liking, such as message length and whether the message contained an image or not.

Also, we explored the role of other potential predictors of message liking, such as message length and whether the message contained an image or not.

sections.paper_id: Schlicht et al. - 2022 - Arranging the fruit basket A computational approach towards a better understanding of adolescents'
paragraphs: Out of the created diet-related dictionary (N = 1943), 195 unique dietary items were mentioned in adolescents' messages. In total 907 messages received a nutri-score. Measures of central tendency of message healthiness (from -2: very unhealthy to 2: very healthy) were at the midpoint of the scale (M = -0.03, SD = 1.23), suggesting no general preference for either healthy or unhealthy foods or drinks.

To assess message healthiness more precisely, we considered the non-normal distribution of nutri-scores within the diet-related dictionary. After all, when certain nutri-score values are overrepresented in the dictionary they have a higher possibility to be found in adolescents' messages. This was addressed by calculating the difference between the percental share of healthiness values in the dictionary and the percental share of values found within the dataset. Numbers below zero henceforth indicated an underrepresentation of the given healthiness value (compared to the dictionary), while numbers above zero indicated a relative overrepresentation.

Fig. 1 shows that neutral to unhealthy foods and drinks (values 0 and -1), were over-proportionally discussed in adolescents' messages (i.e., +6 % and + 12 % respectively). Both the regression line (green) and the more detailed polygonal frequency line (orange) show an overall negative trend, with more healthy items being relatively underrepresented. This suggests that when correcting for the distribution in the dictionary, adolescents indeed showed a preference to communicate more frequently about neutral (e.g., sausage, nut-snacks) to rather unhealthy (e.g., burger, chocolate mousse, marshmallow) dietary items. Further, the polygonal trend indicates that the extreme values very healthy (e.g., "kale", "salad", "spinach") and very unhealthy ("Oreos", "Twix", "lemonade") were relatively less often mentioned. To some degree this finding might be attributable to the way the message healthiness score was calculated (see Method section), resulting in a regression to the mean effect.

This was addressed by calculating the difference between the percental share of healthiness values in the dictionary and the percental share of values found within the dataset. Numbers below zero henceforth indicated an underrepresentation of the given healthiness value (compared to the dictionary), while numbers above zero indicated a relative overrepresentation.

Fig. 1 shows that neutral to unhealthy foods and drinks (values 0 and -1), were over-proportionally discussed in adolescents' messages (i.e., +6 % and + 12 % respectively). Both the regression line (green) and the more detailed polygonal frequency line (orange) show an overall negative trend, with more healthy items being relatively underrepresented. This suggests that when correcting for the distribution in the dictionary, adolescents indeed showed a preference to communicate more frequently about neutral (e.g., sausage, nut-snacks) to rather unhealthy (e.g., burger, chocolate mousse, marshmallow) dietary items. Further, the polygonal trend indicates that the extreme values very healthy (e.g., "kale", "salad", "spinach") and very unhealthy ("Oreos", "Twix", "lemonade") were relatively less often mentioned. To some degree this finding might be attributable to the way the message healthiness score was calculated (see Method section), resulting in a regression to the mean effect.

Measures of central tendency of message healthiness (from -2: very unhealthy to 2: very healthy) were at the midpoint of the scale (M = -0.03, SD = 1.23), suggesting no general preference for either healthy or unhealthy foods or drinks.

paragraphs: To identify broader latent topics in adolescents' diet-related messages (N = 2138), LDA topic modeling was performed. Out of the identified 50 topics, the 15 most dominant topics accounted for 94 % of all dietary messages in the dataset. Therefore, the subsequent analysis focused on these 15 topics with residual topics coded as topic 16. At face validity, some meaningful topics emerged from these 15 topics. In the most prevalent topic (topic 1), adolescents communicated that they needed to leave the chat 'to go to eat', or about their plans what they were 'going to eat' (most common words: "eten" [eating], "gaan" [to go], "eerst" [first] Examples: "Sorry ik moet net eten…" [Sorry I just have to eat] Or: "Ik ga vanavond pizza eten" [I am going to eat pizza tonight]). The second-largest topic was concerned with the feeling of hunger (Example: "Hier krijg ik zo'n honger van" [This makes me so hungry]. In contrast, adolescents almost never communicated about the feeling of satiety after a meal. Topic modeling also revealed the social context of eating, with topic 3 being about dietary behaviors together with family ("zameneten" [eating together], "wachten" [to wait], "familie" [family], " […] we vieren vandaag suikerfeest bij me oma alweer en het word gezellig heel veel familie bij elkaar en gaan we lekker koekjes enzo eten" [[…] We are celebrating sugar festival at my grandmother's again today and it will be nice with lots of family together and we will eat biscuits and stuff.]). Topic 11 references the social context of eating by referring to eating/drinking at school ("Voor schoolreis op school snoep menemen" [Bringing sweets for the school trip to school]. These topics corresponded to diet-related social media themes identified in previous qualitative research (e.g., Vidal et al., 2015).

Other topics found in the analysis were either very specific, with single food-words accounting for a whole topic (i.e., topic 4: cake, topic 6: chicken, topic 7: French fries, or topic 12: cheese), or failing to show one single identifiable theme (topic 8-11, and topic 13-15). Therefore, topic modeling proved only moderately useful for detecting dietary themes and gaining clearer insights into message contents. Overall, 4 topics (out of 15) appeared meaningfully distinct, while the remaining topics were either on different levels of specificity (i.e., regarding single food topics) or remained ambiguous to the coder.

sections.paper_id: Schlicht et al. - 2022 - Arranging the fruit basket A computational approach towards a better understanding of adolescents'
paragraphs: When only considering dietary messages that did express any indication of sentiment or subjectivity 628 messages remained in the subsample. The average sentiment score throughout the subsample was positive (M = 0.25, SD = 0.47, Fig. 2). This means that messages were formulated in a rather positive tone of voice, indicated by words like "good", "nice", and "tasty". Measures of central tendency revealed a high level of subjectivity (M = 0.78, SD = 0.22, Fig. 2), showing that adolescents communicated most frequently from their perspective (i.e., indicated by words like "I" or "me"). These findings suggest that adolescents predominantly communicated about what they did like (vs. what they did not like) with regards to diet and mostly reported from their personal dietary experiences or perceptions.

To assess if message sentiment and subjectivity differed in relation to message healthiness, two bivariate correlations were run. Both message sentiment (r = -0.04, p = .194) and subjectivity (r = -0.01, p = .744) did not show a statistically significant association with message healthiness. Therefore, no evidence was found that message sentiment or subjectivity differed for more healthy or more unhealthy diet-related messages.

paragraphs: As the second measure of linguistic style, six psychological dimensions from the LIWC tool were assessed (affection, socialness, Fig. 2. Distribution of message sentiment and message subjectivity Note. n = 628 (without neutral scores).

drives, and cognitive, perceptual, and biological focus). Descriptive statistics showed that the majority of messages received zero-scores by the LIWC tool, not because of the neutrality of the message, but rather because of the lack of any identifiable word within it (percentages of zero-scores: affection: 78 %, social processes: 62 %, cognitive processes: 65 %, perceptual processes: 83 %, biological processes: 26 %, drives: 82 %). Biological processes were considerably more often referred to, given that words like "eten" [eating] or "drinken" [drinking] were among the most common words in the diet-related sample. The means of affection (M = 18.05, SD = 15.81, n = 461), socialness (M = 18.43, SD = 9.86, n = 807), cognitive focus (M = 19.84, SD = 10.25, n = 755), perceptual focus (M = 17.34, SD = 15.87, n = 365), drives (M = 15.47, SD = 12.66, n = 376) did not indicate that one of these dimensions was more strongly referred to. Non-surprisingly, biological processes (i.e., eating and drinking) were more strongly represented in adolescents' diet-related messages (M = 26.36, SD = 20.00, n = 1592).

Six bivariate correlations tested possible associations between the six psychological dimensions and message healthiness. The analyses revealed a small positive association between message affection and message healthiness (r = 0.07, p = .043), indicating a slightly higher level of affection (absolute value of expressed emotions) in more healthy messages. No other correlations reached statistical significance.

sections.paper_id: Schlicht et al. - 2022 - Arranging the fruit basket A computational approach towards a better understanding of adolescents'
paragraphs: To investigate the relation between message content (message healthiness and message topics, and the number of likes that a message received, two linear regression models were run. The first regression model (see Table 1) with message healthiness as the independent variable and the number of message likes as the dependent variable did not yield significant results. Therefore, no support was found that the healthiness of foods/drinks mentioned within a message predicts the number of likes that a message receives. Hypothesis 1 was not supported.

The second regression model (see Table 2) investigated the relation between the 15 identified message topics as the independent variable and the number of message likes as the dependent variable. The nominal variable was recoded using deviation coding. This allowed us to compare the number of likes of a specific topic, against the mean number of likes of all other topics. Topic 16, which contained all messages not captured by the 15 most dominant topics, functioned as the reference category. Deviation coding requires an adjustment of the significance threshold due to possible correlations between predictors (Field, 2019). When adjusting the significance threshold via Bonferroni correction (adjusted p = .003), no single topic significantly predicted message likes. Therefore, no evidence was found that any specific topic received a higher number of likes than the grand mean.

sections.paper_id: Schlicht et al. - 2022 - Arranging the fruit basket A computational approach towards a better understanding of adolescents'
paragraphs: To investigate the relation between linguistic style (message sentiment, message subjectivity, psychological dimensions) and the number of likes that a message received, two linear regression models were run. The regression model with message sentiment and subjectivity as independent variables (see Table 3), and the number of message likes as the dependent variable was significant, F (4, 2133) = 9.30, p > .001, showing that the overall model was successful in predicting message liking. The model showed weak, positive associations between message sentiment and message liking, and between subjectivity and message liking. This suggests that diet-related messages in a more positive and more subjective tone, receive slightly more likes. These findings support Hypothesis 2.

The regression model with the six psychological dimensions as independent variables and the number of likes that a message received as the dependent variable reached statistical significance, F (8, 2129) = 2.34, p = .017 (Table 4). Therefore, the model was significantly better at predicting message likes than the null model. However, no independent variable on its own significantly predicted message liking, indicating a too weak association for the individual predictors. Although the psychological dimensions combined might have predictive power, these analyses did not provide evidence that any single dimension can predict the number of likes that a message received. To investigate the relation between linguistic style (message sentiment, message subjectivity, psychological dimensions) and the number of likes that a message received, two linear regression models were run. The regression model with message sentiment and subjectivity as independent variables (see Table 3), and the number of message likes as the dependent variable was significant, F (4, 2133) = 9.30, p > .001, showing that the overall model was successful in predicting message liking. The model showed weak, positive associations between message sentiment and message liking, and between subjectivity and message liking. This suggests that diet-related messages in a more positive and more subjective tone, receive slightly more likes. These findings support Hypothesis 2.

The regression model with the six psychological dimensions as independent variables and the number of likes that a message received as the dependent variable reached statistical significance, F (8, 2129) = 2.34, p = .017 (Table 4). Therefore, the model was significantly better at predicting message likes than the null model. However, no independent variable on its own significantly predicted message liking, indicating a too weak association for the individual predictors. Although the psychological dimensions combined might have predictive power, these analyses did not provide evidence that any single dimension can predict the number of likes that a message received. sections.paper_id: Schlicht et al. - 2022 - Arranging the fruit basket A computational approach towards a better understanding of adolescents'
paragraphs: The present study examined the content (i.e., message healthiness, message topics) and linguistic style (i.e., sentiment and psychological dimensions) of adolescents' diet-related social media communications. Moreover, the relationship between message content, linguistic style, and the number of likes that a message received was assessed as an indicator of potential social influence regarding diet.

Approximately 3 % of adolescents' communications revolved around dietary content, which was slightly less than expected from previous studies (Holmberg et al., 2016). However, given the sheer volume of consumed social media content (~ 6 h of daily internet use for European adolescents; Ortiz-Ospina, 2019) these findings still support the role of social media as a considerable source of dietary social influence for adolescents (Holmberg et al., 2016;Vaterlaus et al., 2015). The present study made use of a tailor-made social media platform that allowed adolescent's to exchange direct and group messages, similar to popular direct messaging applications like WhatsApp or FacebookMessenger. Moreover a function was added that allowed users to give messages a "Like". Therefore, the present results might most accurately translate to the mentioned direct messaging platforms. The current study hereby adds to the current literature, as most of the present literature on adolescents' dietary communication has focused on more one-to-many social media platforms like Instagram or Facebook (Holmberg et al., 2016;Qutteina et al., 2019). However, this also means, that we might have obtained different results on a social media platform that more strongly resembles the beforementioned.

The analysis of message content revealed a slight preference of adolescents to communicate about neutral to unhealthy content (based on the nutri-score), which accorded with previous studies). To some degree the term "neutral" for a nutri-score of "C" might be euphemistic in the current case, considering that processed foods (e.g., processed meat, sweetened dairy, white flour) are still able to score "C", provided that they are not simultaneously high in fat and high in sugar (Ter Borg et al., 2021). Unhealthy foods may have been deemed somewhat more worthy of sharing online, which corresponded with the theme of hedonistic indulgence identified by Vidal et al. (2015). As suggested by Holmberg et al. (2016) adolescents might be subconsciously emulating the dietary content they observe around them, including the immense quantity of food marketing (Folkvord et al., 2016;Qutteina et al., 2019) Therefore, the present results might be a result of years of unhealthy food Note. N = 2137. R 2 = 0.01. CI = confidence interval; LL = lower limit; UL = upper limit. a 0 = male, 1 = female. marketing, shaping adolescents dietary norms on social media (Folkvord et al., 2016;Qutteina et al., 2019). Such a trend is concerning: it is entirely conceivable that social media communications reflect the dietary preferences of users (Abbar et al., 2015;Fried et al., 2014). Yet, the interplay between online and offline communication might result in a reinforcing spiral, with unhealthy dietary items becoming more dominant on both social media and in real life. Moreover, latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) revealed 15 broader message topics within the communication data. Four topics (i.e., going to eat, hunger, social context, and school) overlapped with previous findings on dietary themes in adolescents' social media communications (Vidal et al., 2015). However, other topics were specific (e.g., "Topics: "cake" or "chicken") or did not yield an identifiable theme. They gave little insight into adolescents' message content and were more abstract. Moreover, none of the topics were meaningfully related to the number of likes that a message received. LDA, therefore, offered only limited benefits for categorizing dietary social media messages and understanding potential influence dynamics. Most importantly LDA appeared not well suited for short-length social media text as in the present study.

The analysis of linguistic style (i.e., message sentiment, subjective, and psychological dimensions) revealed that the adolescents had a slight preference to communicate about diet with positive valence and from a subjective perspective. These results align with previous findings, that adolescents tend to communicate more strongly from a subjective, personal perspective (Siriaraya et al., 2011), while readily expressing their emotions (Jang et al., 2016). We did not find a difference in sentiment between healthier (vs. unhealthier) dietary messages, which indicates that both healthy and unhealthy food items were discussed in an equally positive tone. On the one hand, these findings can give hope, showing that when adolescents discussed healthy foods, they tended to do this within a positive context, indicating a positive attitude towards these foods (de Ridder & Gillebaart, 2022). This is good news for future dietary interventions, indicating that healthy dietary attitudes already exist, and can potentially be strengthened. On the other hand, the current results also show that unhealthy dietary messages were discussed in an overall larger quantity and with an equally positive tone. This surplus of unhealthy dietary content might currently overshadow more healthy influences, by setting the dominant norm within the network (Holmberg et al., 2016;Powell et al., 2015;Valente, 2012).

The present study investigated the extent to which different dimensions of message content and linguistic style might have explained the number of likes that a message received as a measure of its potential dietary influence. Messages with a more positive sentiment and higher subjectivity received slightly more likes from peers. This corresponds to previous findings that messages that elicit positive emotion tend to receive more likes (Santini et al., 2020;Klassen et al., 2018a). By contrast, there was no evidence that the exact content (i.e., message topics) mattered in terms of message likes. This suggests that it might not so much be the content itself but rather the linguistic style that predicted message engagement.

Contrary to our expectations, adolescents did also give more likes to unhealthy content than to more healthy content. This was counterintuitive, given that adolescents' preferences for unhealthier dietary food and content have been well established (Barre et al., 2016;Folkvord et al., 2021;Holmberg et al., 2016). On the one hand, it might be that these results were an artifact of the used social media platform, that resembled instant messaging platforms like WhatsApp or Facebook Messenger. The newly added "Like" function might not have been as readily adopted by adolescents in the study. On the other hand, the findings might also suggest that there is indeed a difference between adolescents (re-)producing unhealthy social media norms in terms of quantity (Folkvord et al., 2021;Holmberg et al., 2016), while at the same time holding more balanced, moderate attitudes regarding healthy/unhealthy dietary items as currently expected (de Ridder & Gillebaart, 2022).

Finally, an exploratory analysis combined all dimensions of message content and linguistic style in one model and investigated other potential predictors of message liking (i.e., message length and images). The analysis confirmed previous results -that message content could not predict message liking, but linguistic style (i.e., message subjectivity) might. Furthermore, the length of the message did not predict more message likes, suggesting that the length implies strength heuristic (Chaiken, 1987) might not hold in the context of adolescents' social media communications. However, we did find convincing evidence that the visual content of social media messages (i.e., whether they contained an image or not) was more closely correlated with message liking. This is in line with the dominant view about the importance of visuality on social media for engagement (e.g., Manic, 2015). Also, it is been suggested that images supplementing text trigger more effortless cognitive information processing (Moreno & Mayer, 2004). This might be particularly relevant for adolescents, who are still developing their cognitive resources (Mills et al., 2015). Future researchers might investigate whether cognitive mechanisms such as processing fluency or cognitive load might precede higher engagement rates with messages that have corresponding images.

sections.paper_id: Schlicht et al. - 2022 - Arranging the fruit basket A computational approach towards a better understanding of adolescents'
paragraphs: The present study adds to the understanding of adolescents' dietary communications. It was conducted using a dataset of real-life communication data and high-quality dictionaries (i.e., for the assessment of message healthiness), which gave the findings high ecological validity, and a variety of computational methods. Not only did the study investigate what was communicated (i.e., content and healthiness) but also how it was communicated (i.e., sentiment and psychological dimensions). The latter has been under-researched. It is hoped that the methodological and theoretical insights gained might be helpful for future studies, especially those dealing with similar big data communication datasets. To the best of our knowledge, it is the first study to investigate several message dimensions relating to the influence potential of a dietary message (i.e., message liking). The results are very relevant to the current social media landscape. Social media algorithms (i.e., Facebook and Instagram) are known to prioritize messages with high engagement metrics (i.e., likes) for other users' feeds (Morgan, 2019;Oremus et al., 2021). Therefore, studying the message characteristics that are associated with more likes (and therefore have higher visibility) is critical in understanding dietary social influence dynamics on social media.

The study and the used dataset itself have several limitations. First of all, we used automated content analysis to gain insight into the messages within our dataset. While this methodology brings some benefits (e.g., regarding scaling), it also comes with limitations. Generally, automated text analysis can struggle to pick up the context of a message and generally fails in picking up non-literal languages like irony or metaphors. We accounted for this limitation to some degree, by also analyzing linguistic style for establishing message context. Nonetheless, we are aware that automated context analysis can hardly reach the semantic understanding of human coders.

Further, the messages in the dataset were relatively short, with an average of eight words. After stopword removal and further filtering steps in the case of topic modeling, message length decreased even further (M = 3.03, SD = 4.58). Although the length of messages was likely a realistic representation of adolescents' social media communications, it posed problems for the used computational methods. Methods such as sentiment analysis, linguistic inquiry and word count analysis, and LDA become more nuanced and reliable when longer text samples are being examined (Boumans & Trilling, 2016). In particular topic modeling proved to be sensitive to the limited word input, struggling to discover meaningful topics between messages. This inherent limitation of computational topic modeling should be kept in mind for future studies that deal with comparable short-length social media data.
Moreover, the message healthiness score might have been slightly

biased by a regression to the mean effect, though this did not alter the overall trend of the message healthiness results. Overall, the nutri-score system was considered a superior measure of nutritional healthiness compared with the commonly used caloric measure (Dréano-Trécant et al., 2020;Finkelstein et al., 2019). Finally, the study used message likes as an indicator of a message's influence potential (Hamshaw et al., 2018;Hawkins et al., 2021;Lutfeali et al., 2020). Nonetheless, no definite claims can be made about social media influence effeccts on actual dietary behavior, as this was not measured within the present study.

sections.paper_id: Schlicht et al. - 2022 - Arranging the fruit basket A computational approach towards a better understanding of adolescents'
paragraphs: Taking into account these limitations, three important conclusions can be drawn (each of which has implications for research and practice). First, the present study offered support for previous findings, that adolescents discuss more unhealthy dietary content (i.e., foods with nutriscores of C or worse) more often than healthier content (nutri-scores of A or B). This is concerning because the healthiness of dietary online content has been shown to be an accurate reflection users' dietary preferences and risk for obesity (Abbar et al., 2015;Fried et al., 2014). Worse, the interplay between social media influences and dietary attitudes and behaviors might create a reinforcing spiral, with unhealthy eating patterns consolidating themselves both online and offline.

Second, the results suggest that it might not be so much the content of the message (i.e., healthiness and message topic) that determines message liking but rather its linguistic style; more positive sentiments and subjectivity were associated with slightly more message liking. In general, adolescents tended to discuss healthy and unhealthy dietary matters more from their perspective and more in a positive tone. These findings indicate a need to educate adolescents further on what a healthy diet consists of and how they can benefit from healthy dietary practices. Also, dietary interventions should pay close attention to the tone they use to formulate their intervention messages to maximize engagement and possibly persuasion outcomes.

Third, the results showed that messages that contained images, and in particular dietary images, received considerably more likes from peers. The highest number of likes were observed when text and image were congruent (i.e., they both concerned dietary content). Future researchers might further investigate the relationship between text and image and their effects on message liking; investigating the cognitive mechanisms of content liking might lead to a better understanding of both.

Overall, the findings stress the need for effective dietary interventions in youth populations. Understanding adolescents' dietary communication preferences are critical for designing more effective interventions that engage adolescents long-term and promote healthier dietary practices (Klassen et al., 2018b;Maher et al., 2014;Yang, 2017). To this end, it appears very valuable to involve adolescents themselves for example in the form of co-creation sessions. Co-creation has recently seen increased use in the context of health promotion, and early results are promising (Linda et al., 2021;von Heimburg & Cluley, 2021). Involving adolescents may be a way not only to empower and engage adolescents concerning their dietary choices but also to design dietary interventions that are truly relevant and engaging for them (Foster-Fishman et al., 2010;Linda et al., 2021;Yang, 2017).

To investigate the relation between all variables of message content (message healthiness, message topics), linguistic style (message sentiment, subjectivity, and psychological dimensions), and the number of likes that a message receives (DV) one full regression model was run.
